data.title: Occidental Petroleum Vs. Energy Transfer: Where I Diverge From Berkshire Hathaway
data.description:
I last analyzed Occidental Petroleum Corporation (NYSE:OXY) on May 21 with an article titled "Occidental Petroleum: Why Warren Buffett Kept Buying." That article was triggered by Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.A) (BRK.B) disclosure of its OXY position at that time and rated OXY as a buy. As for Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET), I last rated it on June 25 with an article titled "Energy Transfer: For Those Tired Of The Oil Price Rollercoaster." The article was triggered by the oil price changes caused by OPEC+ production decisions and the Israel-Iran conflict at that time. I also rated ET as a buy.

Since those articles, there have been a few notable developments both relevant to these specific oil stocks and also the oil sector as whole. In the rest of this article, I will focus on the top change as I see in each category. For the former category, I will focus on the disclosure of BRK’s latest holdings. And for the latter category, I will focus on the change - and also my outlook - for oil prices.

BRK recently filed its Form 10-Q for FQ2 2025. Per the disclosure, BRK’s equity portfolio keeps betting heavily on leading oil stocks, with CVX and OXY continuing to be among its top six positions with OXY’s carrying value reported at $16.4 billion (see the first chart below). In addition, BRK has become a 10%-plus shareholder for OXY and has continued to accumulate BRK shares lately (see the next chart below). Besides ownership of common shares, BRK also owns non-voting Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock of Occidental and Occidental common stock warrants, as detailed in its 10-Q below:

BRK FQ2 disclosure: As of June 30, 2025, our investment in Occidental preferred stock had an aggregate liquidation value of approximately $8.5 billion. The Occidental common stock warrants currently allow us to purchase up to 83.9 million shares of Occidental common stock at an exercise price of $59.59 per share. The warrants are exercisable in whole or in part until one year after the date the preferred stock is fully redeemed.

Against this background, next I will explain A) why I share the overall bullish sentiment toward oil stocks, but B) why I see ET as a better bet than OXY given the new developments since my last writing. These considerations have also led me to adjust my OXY rating to hold.

But we like ET better

I share Berkshire's and Warren Buffett’s overall bullish sentiment toward oil stocks for a couple of reasons. In a nutshell, these companies are "cash machines" that are for sale at attractive valuations (and I bet in Buffett’s view too). They keep generating significant free cash flow, which can be used for share buybacks, dividends, and growth reinvestment (more on these later). I also believe the current oil prices are too compressed and anticipate sustained global demand to support oil price recovery for the years to come. Oil prices have risen about 11% since my last writing, as you can see from the following chart, and I keep several multiple drivers to support even higher oil prices. These drivers are detailed in my recent article (e.g., refilling of Strategic Petroleum Reserve, inflation, low inventory, etc.), and I won’t repeat the details here anymore.

Here I will just build on those factors to argue that if/when oil prices continue recovering, Energy Transfer is better positioned than Occidental Petroleum Corp. ET is primarily a midstream stock. For readers new to the jargon, this means it earns money mainly by serving as the middleman in energy world, by storing and moving oil and natural gas for other players. As such, common sense is that ET should be relatively insensitive to oil price changes than integrated oil companies like OXY, which directly drill and produce oil.

However, as a contrarian, I'm skeptical about this common sense (and pretty much all common senses in general). Both my past experiences with oil stocks and also historical data seem to contradict this notion. As an example, the chart below displays the historical correlation between Brent Crude prices and the total amount of dividends paid by OXY (top panel) and ET (bottom panel) in the past 10 years. Here, I'm using their dividends as a measure of their long-term owners’ earnings. As seen, both companies show a positive correlation on average, which should come as no surprise. However, quantitatively, OXY shows a moderate correlation coefficient of 0.145 on average, distinctly weaker than the average correlation of 0.244 for ET.

Why? There are couple of reasons that could make ET equally positioned – or better positioned – to capitalize on higher oil prices. It's certainly true that ET’s earnings are derived from fee-based contracts, and these contracts are not directly linked to spot oil and/or natural gas prices. But the operative word here is “directly” because oil prices can – and do – play a role indirectly through many other channels. Here I will go over the top channels briefly. To start, high energy prices (either oil or natural gas prices) motivate producers like OXY to produce more, which of course only creates higher demand for midstream players like ET. Such a demand can translate into higher transportation volumes flowing through ET’s network of pipelines and a better pricing position for ET. Additionally, besides increased volume, higher energy prices often also mean better financed customers for ET. When energy prices increase, ET’s customer base (which again includes oil and gas producers) enjoys wider profit margins, which decreases the odds for them to default on their contracts and/or increases the odds for them to renew contracts.

ET offers a better total return potential

Besides the above historical correlation data, the changes in their ROCE (return on capital employed) serve as another illustration of my above arguments. The calculation of ROCE here is based on operating cash flow (to minimize the swing of accounting EPS) and considers the following items as capital employed: Working capital (inventory, payables, receivables), PPE (plants, properties, and equipment). As seen in the next two charts, both companies suffered a significant decline in their ROCE amid the COVID pandemic. OXY’s decline was far more drastic than ET. Its ROCE bottomed at 5.3% only in the first quarter of 2021, far below its historical average of around (12%) and also the average of the energy sector (approximated by XLE, which is ~14.1%).

As the impacts of the pandemic fade, both also enjoyed a recovery in their ROCE. However, OXY’s recovery halted in Q4 of 2022 after its ROCE peaked at 25.2% on a TTM basis. Our estimate for its 2025 FWD ROCE is around 20.3%. In contrast, as seen in the second chart, ET’s recovery continued with our 2025 estimate pointing to a ROCE of 18.6%, quite close to OXY.

Despite the similar FWD ROCE shown in our model, OXY now trades at a far higher P/E multiple than ET as you can see from the following screenshot. To wit, OXY stock is currently trading at an FY1 P/E of almost 18x, compared to about 13.3x only for ET.

Such a divergence in valuation, when combined with their similar FWD ROCE, has led us to see a far better return potential for ET as illustrated in the next chart below. The detailed mechanism behind this chart is described in full in our earlier article. As such, only the end results are quoted here:

  • The long-term ROI for a business owner is simply determined by two things: A) the price paid to buy the business and B) the quality of the business. More specifically, part A is determined by the owner’s earning yield (“OEY”) when we purchased the business. And that is why P/E is the first dimension in the chart. Part B is determined by the quality of the business and that is why ROCE (return on capital employed) is the second dimension in the chart.
  • The long-term earnings growth rate is estimated by the product of the ROCE and the Reinvestment Rate.
  • Finally, long-term ROI = valuation + quality = OEY + Growth Rate = OEY + ROCE*Reinvestment Rate

With ET and OXY’s valuation ratios and our ROCE projection, we see a total ROI potential of around 9%, noticeably above our projection of around 7% from OXY assuming a 10% growth reinvestment rate.

Other risks and final thoughts

If you subscribe to our above approach, then another upside risk for ET is that a large portion of its total return potential is supported by its dividend already. To wit, the chart below compares the current and historical dividend yields of ET and OXY. As seen, ET features a forward dividend yield of 7.39% (vs. OXY's 2.26% and our total return projection of ~9%). The difference becomes even more pronounced when we expand the horizon and examine the four-year average yield. ET's average yield is about 7.41% in the past four years (quite consistent with its current level), while OXY's average yield is only 1.01% due to the dividend cuts (to a quarterly payout of 1 cent per share) amid the pandemic.

In terms of downside risks, ET’s heavier reliance on debt financing is a key concern for us. Based on their balance sheet data as of the most recent quarter below, OXY features better liquidity in the short term and also a more conservative balance sheet in the long term. For example, OXY currently holds about $2.61B in total cash. In contrast, ET holds about $453 million only despite their similar market caps (about $61 billion for ET and $42 billion for OXY). ET also carries a much heavier debt load, with total debt at $60.61B, compared to OXY's total debt of $25.99B. As a net results of these differences, ET’s leverage (measured in total debt to equity ratio) is quite elevated at over 130%, almost double OXY’s 74%.

To recap, this update on ET and OXY is mainly triggered by the latest disclosure of BRK and also oil price changes since my last analyses. These developments have led me to keep agreeing with Berkshire’s bullish sentiment toward oil stocks in general. But in terms of the specific choices, they led me to a better return/risk ratio in ET than in OXY. In particular, the analysis of their ROCE and total return potential, a tool we use in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) to provide a broader context, shows a gap wide enough for us to adjust our OXY rating to Hold.

data.title: Celsius: Alani Home Run
data.description:
In not a huge shock here, Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) reported a blockbuster Q2 after adding in the booming Alani Nu business. My investment thesis remains Bullish on the stock, even after the rally to $50+ due to the growth opportunity still ahead.

2 Is Better Than 1

Celsius reported a monster quarter after including the financials of Alani Nu in the business. The acquisition closed back on April 1 and the Q2'25 results were the first quarter to include the new business as follows:

  • Non-GAAP EPS of $0.47 beats by $0.23.
  • Revenue of $739.3 million (+83.9% YoY) beats by $84.97 million.

The big question on revenues after an acquisition is whether analyst estimates correctly captured the revenue addition from the combined company. Celsius hit $402 million in Q2'24 sales and $329 million in Q1'25 standalone revenues.

The business hit $739 million in Q2'25 for growth of $337 million YoY. Alani Nu supposedly hit the $1 billion sales level for the period ending April 13 and only reported 2024 sales of $600 million.

Alani Q2 sales were $301 million while Celsius grew 9% YoY to reach $438 million. Analysts only forecast the addition of $252 million in sales while Alani clearly appeared on a far faster path alone. Either way, the business is running at a possible $3 billion run rate for a combined business that produced 2024 sales of only $2.0 billion.

The new Celsius controls 17.3% of the U.S. RTD energy market. The business would seem on a path to control 30% of the market similar to Red Bull and Monster Beverage (MNST).

Monster is forecast to report $2.1 billion in Q2 sales with their U.S. RTD market share slipping below 27%. As well, the energy drink company obtains ~40% of sales, or $3 billion, from international markets

Celsius is still building out the international operations with Q2'25 sales of just $25 million. The Alani acquisition adds nothing to international expansion.

Big Upside

The consensus analyst estimates only forecast the business reaches $2.7 billion in sales next year and $3.05 billion in 2027. Note, the 2025 numbers exclude the Q1 sales of Alani, which around $250 million would boost the pro-forma sales targets for 2025 to $2.45 billion for a baseline target.

Celsius is likely to see these targets hiked over the next week after the big Q2 beat. Alani sales alone grew 129% in the quarter to reach the $301 million level.

Up at $50, the stock has a market cap of $13 billion. Celsius is still relatively reasonably valued at 4x sales updated forward sales estimates of likely $3 billion while the stock regularly traded closer to a valuation of 8x similar to Monster Beverage (MNST).

The current forward EV/S sales target have Celsius far cheaper at just 5x targets versus Monster Beverage up at 7x. Celsius is growing at a pro-forma clip of ~30% while Monster is forecast to just grow at a 10% clip.

Celsius is the far cheaper play with the faster growth. The company even produced $210 million in adjusted EBITDA in the quarter for an EPS of $0.47, double analyst estimates.

The company boosted EPS $0.19 YoY and Celsius hasn't even made progress on the $50 million in deal synergies. At $50, the company is likely trading at a cheap valuation compared to the opportunity to now power ahead towards a $2 EPS.

As usual, Celsius hit a growth roadblock prior to adding Alani and the combined company could end up facing pressure from another healthy energy drink player, or the market could just shift to another drink category. As well, the energy drink company could fail to successfully expand in Europe and other locations with different tastes and interests.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the market still needs to adjust to the combined financials after the big Alani boost. Even Celsius is reporting better numbers without distribution inventory adjustments hurting reported sales.

Investors need to realize Celsius has a market cap of just $13 billion and Monster Beverage is up at a whopping $59 billion, outlining an easy target for the energy drink company over time.

data.title: Is Manulife Still A 'Buy' Following Its Q2 2025 Earnings?
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, I see Manulife Financial Corporation (NYSE:MFC) as a good long-term income investment in the insurance industry, as the company has strong business fundamentals and good growth prospects due to its large exposure to Asia.

Following a share price dip a few months ago related to general market weakness following President Trump’s tariffs, I switched my recommendation to Buy, as Manulife’s valuation and dividend yield were more attractive to long-term investors. Its shares have rebounded since then, but have underperformed the overall stock market during the same period, as shown in the next graph.

Regarding Manulife’s most recent financial performance, the company has released yesterday its financial figures related to Q2 2025, reporting a mixed performance as new business value and sales have grown quite well, but earnings were practically flat compared to the same quarter of last year.

Indeed, Manulife’s Annual Premium Equivalent (APE) sales were up 15% YoY to more than CAD $2.2 billion in the quarter, with strong growth achieved both in Asia and the U.S., while APE sales were down in Canada. Beyond higher APE sales, its new business value (NBV) was up 20% to CAD $846 million, being driven mainly by growth in Asia.

In the Global Wealth Management segment, it reported net inflows of close to CAD $1 billion during the quarter, which can be considered a positive outcome considering that capital markets were quite volatile following the announcement of Trump’s tariffs back in April, a background that could have been negative for net inflows due to higher risk-aversion from clients. Indeed, there was some net outflows from retail clients, which usually are more sensitive to market swings, that were offset by net inflows in the institutional and retirement accounts.

Despite a positive operating momentum regarding its top-line, Manulife’s earnings were impacted by higher claims in the U.S. life business, leading to higher-than-expected costs related to mortality. The company sees this as normal volatility in claims rather than an unfavorable mortality trend.

Regarding its investment portfolio, the company also booked some credit losses, even though it continues to expect credit losses (ECL) of between CAD $30-50 million on average per quarter. In Q2 2025, its ECL provisions amounted to CAD $102 million related to some below investment grade exposures in the U.S., which impacted negatively its earnings given that it compares with a reversal of CAD $1 million booked in the same quarter of 2024. While this is a setback for Manulife’s profitability and earnings growth, its investment portfolio is 96% allocated to investment grade ratings and Manulife is still guiding for CAD $30-50 million of ECL provisions per quarter, showing that it remains confident on the overall quality of its credit exposures.

Nevertheless, these unfavorable developments in the U.S. were the main headwinds for earnings growth during the last quarter, leading to total core earnings of CAD $1.7 billion in the quarter, being slightly lower than compared to Q2 2024. Due to share buybacks performed over the past few quarters, its total number of shares outstanding have declined compared to the same period of last year, resulting on core EPS growth of 2% YoY. This is a relatively weak growth rate that doesn’t reflect the company’s growth prospects over the long term, while adjusted for the negative impact of ECL provisions its core EPS growth would have been 7% during the last quarter.

Its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the insurance sector, was 15.6% in Q2 2025, a very good level of profitability. The company continues to be on the right path to achieve a core ROE above 18% by 2027, as top-line momentum has been good in recent quarters, boding well for earnings growth ahead. Regarding its balance sheet, Manulife continues to have a strong position measured by its leverage ratio below 25% and its LICAT ratio of 136%, allowing it to maintain its capital return policy and also allocate capital towards acquisitions when the opportunity arises.

Together with its earnings, Manulife also announced the acquisition of a 75% stake in Comvest Credit Partners for $937.5 million, with a predefined path to acquire the remaining 25% over the next six years. This deal will boost its assets under management by some $14.7 billion related to private credit assets in the global wealth management segment, and it’s expected to be accretive immediately for its core EPS, ROE, and EBITDA margin following the integration of the business. This deal should have a negative impact of three percentage points on Manulife’s LICAT ratio, not putting in jeopardy its share buyback program ahead. This acquisition is expected to help Manulife achieve higher business growth in private credit by combining its existing teams with Comvest’s expertise, leading also to higher earnings diversification across the global wealth management segment.

By segment, Manulife reported strong business growth in Asia during the second quarter of the year, remaining its major growth driver. As shown in the next graph, the company achieved double-digit growth across most key performance indicators, leading to net income of CAD $600 million in the quarter, representing annual growth of 42% on a reported basis, while its core earnings increased by 13% YoY.

Across Asia, the company is exposed to several countries, but Hong Kong is the most important region representing about half of its new business value and core earnings. Other regions that also had an important contribution to growth were Mainland China and Singapore, while Japan on the other hand had a weaker performance lately.

Other segments, namely global wealth management and Canada also reported a positive operating performance and higher core earnings, while on the negative side its U.S. operations reported a significant drop on earnings due to unfavorable mortality and higher ECL provisions, leading to a net income of only CAD $26 million in the last quarter (vs. CAD $98 million in Q2 2024).

Regarding capital returns, Manulife has returned some CAD $6.4 billion over the last year to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks, while it also was able to grow its adjusted book value at the same time, to CAD $35.78 per share at the end of last quarter.

This shows that Manulife has been able to generate value for shareholders and returned significant excess capital at the same time, showing that its business has very good cash flow generation and a solid financial position, boding well for its business and dividend growth ahead. Manulife has increased its quarterly dividend to CAD $0.44 per share at the beginning of the year, representing an increase of 10% from the previous one. Going forward, according to analysts’ estimates, its dividend is expected to grow by high-single-digits over the next few years, which may be too conservative as the company has good growth prospects and a strong financial position, allowing it to potentially grow its dividend at double-digits over the coming years.

Regarding its valuation, Manulife is currently trading at about 1.6x book value, which is at a premium to its own historical multiple over the last five years (around 1.2x book value). However, as I’ve discussed in previous articles, Manulife has made several efforts to change its business profile in recent years and has made some transactions to reduce its exposure to legacy issues, namely the reduction of risk related to long-term care (LTC) insurance, justifying a higher valuation multiple nowadays compared to some years ago.

For a company that has good growth prospects and is reporting a ROE of about 15%, I see this valuation as attractive. Moreover, compared to some of its life insurance peers, such as Metlife (MET) or Prudential Financial (PFG), it’s also attractive given that its peer group is also trading at around 1.6x book value, but Manulife is a company with better growth prospects due to its large exposure to Asia and global wealth management.

Conclusion

While Manulife’s operating performance in the last quarter can be considered mixed, as positive top-line momentum was offset by some cyclical headwinds in the U.S. life business, the company continues to have strong fundamentals and growth prospects within the life insurance industry. Its acquisition of Comvest also seems to make sense both from a business and financial standpoint, boding well for earnings growth ahead. As the company continues to trade at an attractive valuation and offers a sustainable dividend yield above 4%, I think Manulife remains a Buy in the insurance sector.

data.title: SHYM: A Clever Way To Earn Above-Average Yield With Manageable Risk
data.description:
The details matter, always

Perhaps I shouldn't use the word "trick" here for it's not meant to be an implication that there is some trickery. Rather, that there's been a good and sensible observation here which then leads to a potentially good result for us investors. That sense of trick, not trickery.

That trick - good and sensible observation - requires that we grasp the details of how funds like this are constructed, then what's different with this one.

iShares Short Duration High Yield Muni Active ETF (BATS:SHYM)

So, what we have here is an exchange traded fund - so, an open ended fund - actively trading in the municipal bond market. It's chasing high yield - and therefore presumably low credit rating - bonds and is at the shorter end of the lifespan - or "tenor" - of bonds. Some of this mixture argues for a high income - the bonds will be paying relatively high coupons - and some for low, that timespan. The combination ends up paying what is, for a muni fund, a high distribution. Which is where that trick, that observation, comes in.

Bonds

Bonds are us lending to someone but in a form that is tradeable - it's not necessary to await the repayment in order to get our money back. Bonds come in essentially three flavors; the first is Treasuries. These are lending to Uncle Sam and as he's not gone bust for a couple of centuries now these are regarded as "risk free". That's "credit risk free" by the way for there is still interest rate risk here.

Interest rate risk is that when the underlying interest rate (so, the bank rate, the Fed Funds Rate) changes then new bonds will have to pay a different coupon or interest rate than old ones. Someone who could borrow from us yesterday at 3% will now have to pay 4% as an example. A 3% bond is worth a different capital amount than a 4% one - by about 1% a year for the life of the bond in fact. So a longer tenor bond changes value more when interest rates change than a shorter bond does - whether up or down.

However, it's also true that we get a better interest rate for making a longer term loan than a shorter. So there's always a tension here - in order to get a good income we've got to go out in tenor but that's what also exposes us to more interest rate risk. There isn't, in fact, a cure for this, it's just one of those things. Being able to gain the higher income without taking on that more risk is one of those things we all wish for and yet it's very, very, difficult to find.

The second group of bonds is corporates. Companies can, of course, go bust. So this introduces credit risk. This means that - two risks, not just one - corporates pay higher coupons than Treasuries.

The way to deal with credit risk is through diversification. Have small amounts from many different issuers and the failure of any one of them is annoying but not a disaster. We can usually buy corporate bonds in $1,000 lots at issue, get our money back at redemption. But in the interim, trading secondary bonds, ah, that's more difficult. It depends upon the exact issue, which market, but as an example the European corporate bond markets trade in $200k par value minimum lot sizes. Being well diversified across a couple of hundred issuers in that size well, that's difficult for most of us, right?

The solution to diversification is therefore to be in a fund. The fund is large enough to be diversified, we own a tiny fraction of the fund and we're good.

The third group is municipals and these have both credit and interest rate risk. The credit risk is pretty small, the usual estimate is a default rate of 0.1%. But it's there - so munis should pay more than Treasuries if perhaps less than corporates. Munis do not pay more than Treasuries, they pay less. The reason is that the income from municipal bonds is Federal income tax free (and, usually, instate income tax for instate taxpayers on instate bonds). There are exceptions but we don't need to worry about the AMT here.

Tax equivalent yields

So if munis are tax free and Treasuries and corporates aren't how do we compare the returns from each? For the tax break obviously has a different value for people who pay different rates of tax. The way we do this is the "tax equivalent yield" and the calculation is, well, what would a taxable investment have to pay to equal this non-taxable one dependent upon the tax rate of the taxpayer? Not wholly easy to work out but fortunately people publish tables of this:

It's not possible to be authoritative here for different people have different attitudes to tax. Some really hate it, some think paying at least some is a good idea. But the usual rule of thumb is that if you're paying 24% or more as your marginal rate (the rate on your next $ of income) then you should be seriously thinking about muni bonds.

The details of SHYM

We can see a selection of what they're invested in:

5% in one issuer is a pretty chunky concentration of risk if we're honest with ourselves. We can see the full holdings here.

Fees are 0.35%, which is several times a simple index fund or ETF but pretty reasonable for an actively managed fund. It's an open ended fund so it trades at fractions of a percent off net asset value at any one time. True, there were a couple of days in April when it went as far as a percentage point but that sort of hiccup is rare. Management is by Blackrock so we're with a known name.

320 positions (not issuers) means they're being pretty selective. The muni universe is 6 thousand and larger. The effective duration is pretty short, just under 5 years.

So, given what we know about bonds from up above we'd expect the yield - the distribution rate - to be pretty low. We're in short tenor bonds, munis, so a low coupon and thus a low distribution, right? Except no, we've not that low distribution at all. 4.53% (SEC yield, trailing yield etc, there are different methods of calculating the yield, they'll differ but not by much) is actually pretty good. VTES, an index fund in munis, pays a markedly lower dividend. In fact, if we look at USFR, which is a taxable fund, we have about the same headline yield. True, true, USFR has nearly no interest rate nor credit risk at all, but still. To match a Treasury fund yield with a muni fund is pretty good. Because of course the higher our - our meaning us as the investor - marginal income tax rate, the more that muni yield is worth to us - 7.79% in fact for a top rate taxpayer.

So, how are they outperforming?

The secret is here:

Now "not rated" in the bond markets usually means something like "complete dog and not touching it with your bargepole let alone mine". CCC isn't much better. Actually, in large parts of the bond market BBB isn't thought of as much better than that.

At which point we could just assume that SHYM is achieving high yield by taking on lots of credit risk - the standard complaint, we can only get paid in financial markets by taking risk.

However, the actual default rate in the muni markets is really very low - that 0.1% estimate above. So we could be dealing with comparative credit worthiness not actual. Sure, a BBB muni is less creditworthy than an AAA but if the overall default rate is tiny how much difference does that actually make?

Also, not rated means that the issuer hasn't paid a ratings agency to rate the bond. This is not uncommon for small issues either. Fund managers can still decide upon their own opinion by having their own look at the finances of the issuer. That is, "unrated" does not mean "not worthy of a rating" it means "no one has paid for a rating" which is a very different thing.

Effectively, a fund heavily into unrated bonds is using the calculations of its own managers rather than the calculations of the ratings agencies. Many funds won't touch unrated bonds - so there's a possibility that they're underpriced. Maybe, possibly, we cannot be categorical about this.

That, I think, is the special secret sauce here. Grasping that unrated in the muni market does not mean high credit risk, even if unrated (and other low ratings bonds) pay higher coupons. Thus, without a (substantial that is) increase in risk it is possible to gain higher income. Which is the great trick in any financial market - to get paid for taking risks you're not actually taking, or to be overpaid for the ones you are.

I approve of this - and again, it's not a trick, it's an insight - strategy.

Why I'm wrong

Well, it's always possible that SHYM is just funding an excessive distribution out of return of capital, as so many muni funds in fact do. Except they don't, as we can see here. They are in fact earning that higher distribution.

It's also possible that this is a strategy that hasn't been fully tested yet. This isn't a carry trade but the phrase about the carry trade might apply - picking up nickels in front of a steamroller. It's great until the environment changes and everything turns to.... well, this is a family site but you can imagine what I mean. Everyone ends up with significant problems. This fund has been running since Aug 2021 so hasn't had to go through a full on recession as yet (yes, I know, lockdown, but that was different).

So it is possible that I'm wrong.

My opinion

This comes with all the usual provisos about investing in municipal bonds of course. The tax break means that coupons - interest paid - are lower than other sectors of the bond market. For any given level of risk non-taxpayers are almost certainly better off in the taxable sector as a result.

Further, bonds only make sense for those who are looking for income from their investments. Some, but not all, higher rate taxpayers will be. So, muni bond funds are really only attractive to higher rate taxpayers looking for income.

With those provisos then yes, I recommend SHYM. I think they've found a trick that means the payout is higher than the risk being taken. Please do note that in the version of English that I speak - the English version, not the American - "trick" does not mean trickery. "Ooh, that's a clever trick" has a meaning much closer to "I wish I'd thought of that" than it does to anything else.

Unrated municipal bonds are merely unrated, not uninvestable. From that they're able to gain a higher income stream at little, if any, change in risk level. That's one of those great tricks in financial markets, if you can pull it off, being paid for risks you're not taking.

I recommend.

Again, it's only a recommendation for higher rate taxpayers looking for income - the natural buyers of muni funds. But it's a buy recommendation all the same.

data.title: Meta: Giving Credit To Zuckerberg's AI Blueprint
data.description:
I only started covering Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) on Seeking Alpha in May this year, after Q1 earnings confirmed to me this is a winning AI bet.

However, my journey with Meta as an investor actually began in 2022, when the company’s stock crashed after a couple of bad earnings reports, with investors at the time unconvinced about Meta’s CAPEX investment in questionable “Metaverse” bets.

At that time, I entered a relatively small position in Meta worth $ ~5,000, as I was convinced its role in a de facto online Advertising oligopoly all but guaranteed a turnaround. I held that position until it roughly tripled in value before selling most of it. In hindsight, I shouldn’t have sold, as the company went on outperforming the market. That position would have now returned a ~5X from my 2022 entry price.

What happened to determine such a successful turnaround? I believe the answer lies in CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s long-term vision for his company in the AI race. This is what I will cover in this article.

What is Zuckerberg’s AI vision?

During Q2 earnings, Meta’s CEO outlined five areas of opportunity tied to AI: improved advertising, engaging experiences, business messaging, Meta AI advancements and AI devices. In the next paragraphs, I will go through each of these items outlining how Zuckerberg’s vision evolved in the last few years.

For what concerns “improved advertising,” this is Meta’s ability to improve the effectiveness of its advertising solutions. In simple terms, Meta’s proprietary advertising algorithms are able to increasingly serve better Ads to the right people at the right time, resulting in better conversions and higher ROI for advertisers.

More effective advertising drives more spend per advertiser and more advertisers to Meta’s platforms, resulting in higher growth figures.

This effect is compounded by what Zuckerberg calls “engaging experiences.” This amounts to leveraging AI to make sure that more eyeballs stay on Meta’s platform for longer periods of time. The more people spend on Meta’s platforms, the more (ever-more effective) Ads can be served and the better they can be monetized.

Earlier in 2025, Meta’s noted a 5% year-over-year increase in time spent on Facebook and a 6% increase on Instagram, driven by AI recommendation systems and video engagement (over 20% YoY growth in video time spent on both platforms).

From a financial standpoint, results speak for themselves. Meta’s stock returned almost double what the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq 100 (NDX) did in the last five years. Given that Meta’s revenue is dependent on advertising for 98%+, I believe it is safe to say this performance is the direct result of an Advertising business that has been growing consistently in double digits for a number of years.

Meta has done a great job at leveraging AI to boost its current business - something that is further reflected in Q2 earnings figure, especially for what concerns:

  • Average price per Ad increasing by 9% y-o-y.

  • Overall Ad impressions growing by 11% y-o-y.

All in all, it is clear to me that Meta is increasing both the time people spend on its platforms and the effectiveness of the Ads they are served while there. However, I think the future of the company lies in its other AI bets.

From Meta’s Ads bread and butter to future engines of growth

Business messaging, Meta AI and AI devices are at the core of the future growth of Meta, in my view. The first two are closely intertwined, with Zuckerberg and Meta’s CFO calling out the following new business models for increased monetization:

  • Ads and channel subscriptions roll-out in WhatsApp, a widely used messaging platform with 1.5 Billion users worldwide

  • Growth of advertising in Threads, Meta’s recent social media platform that rivals Elon Musk’s X.

  • Growth of website-to-message ads, directing users to a business’s website before initiating a chat in Meta’s messaging apps.

  • AI-powered business agents that either integrate into ad campaigns or into customers’ e-commerce platforms, likely through paid services or subscriptions.

The latter element is particularly interesting, in my opinion. I think it could enable Meta to have a completely new business model, unrelated to Advertising. Zuckerberg said what follows talking about the future of business AI during the Q2 earnings call (emphasis added):

I’ve talked before about how I believe every business will soon have a business AI, just like they have an e-mail address, social media account and website. We are starting to see some product market fit in a number of countries where we’re testing these agents, and we’re integrating these business AIs into ads on Facebook and Instagram as well as directly into e-commerce websites.

I believe we are still at the early stages of what can be achieved by Meta in terms of leveraging AI. As I discussed in past coverage, I think we are only entering a new AI wave that will be centered on Agentic AI and inference training.

It is in this context that, for the first time, I saw in Meta’s Q2 earnings a possible path to monetizing the large messaging platforms the company has acquired in the past years. More importantly, I saw a path to diversifying the business from Advertising. This is why I am fundamentally bullish on Meta long term, believing Zuckerberg will be able to continue delivering for shareholders.

My bullish sentiment is only compounded by "AI devices," the last element mentioned by Meta's CEO as an area of focus for AI monetization. While we are still in the very early stages, I think this is an interesting bet of Meta on Robotics, which I personally see as the last step in the AI race - with physical AI integrated in our daily lives.

Meta's AR glasses are an interesting take on the world of Augmented Reality. While it is too early to draw conclusions on the TAM for that kind of product (and whether, for example, it could ever replace smartphones), this represents yet another potential future catalyst for the stock.

About Meta’s valuation

At a Forward P/E of ~27 at the time of writing, readers may find Meta expensive. It is true that if compared to other Tech companies, this is a fairly rich valuation. For example, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) trades at below a P/E of 20. That, however, is in my view linked to the threat that AI poses to Search. Unlike Meta, Google has not done a great job at convincing it can truly benefit from AI, and is stuck in a more defensive position - something I covered in my recent article on it about how the web is changing.

Yet, Meta trades at a discount against Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), with a FWD P/E of ~33 at the time of writing. And even against Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), with a similar FWD P/E to that of Microsoft. I think this relative discount is once again due to Meta’s dependence on Advertising. Should the company manage to show a realistic diversification of its business, I believe we could easily see the market pricing in line with Microsoft - representing a ~20% upside from current price levels.

On top of the possibility of a valuation “reset” if it diversifies its business, I expect Meta will keep returning value to shareholders’ organically as a result of accelerated growth in its underlying business. After two quarters of 20%+ revenue growth, I am convinced Meta has unlocked a new pattern of AI-fueled growth.

Overall, I am a strong believer that it is better to pay a premium to buy an excellent business than buying a lousy one at a discount. That’s why I keep rating Meta a BUY, even after a sustained bull run and considering its valuation is at somewhat of a premium. I think it is a no brainer for long term holders who want to be exposed to AI.

Risks

The main risk of an investment in Meta concerns its dependence on Advertising. In case of an economic recession, advertising is usually one of the first industries that are hit as companies cut on their marketing budgets. Meta’s strength - its innovative and highly efficient Ad business - is also its weakness, in that a decline in Advertising spend can hit the business disproportionately when compared to more diversified businesses, such as Microsoft or Amazon.

The company can also be subject to high volatility, being a Tech company that is trading at a fairly rich valuation compared to historical norms (the S&P 500 historical FWD P/E ratio is around 20). I would not recommend a hefty exposure to META for any investor that is not willing to hold long term, and in any case only if such exposure is consistent with the investor’s objectives and risk profile.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Meta’s own brand name is a testament to Mark Zuckerberg’s long term vision for AI. The company was rebranded as Meta in reference to the “Metaverse,” a concept linked to Web3 that was popular in Silicon Valley in the early 2020s. Barely anyone talks about the Metaverse nowadays, with “AI” now used as a catch-all term for anything related to the evolution of the web (including that of social media). I am convinced that if Zuckerberg was to rebrand Meta today, he would choose something AI-related.

Even if the term “AI” was not as used back then as it is today, in the last 4 to 5 years Mark Zuckerberg exhibited what I think amounts to a great long term vision. Results speak for themselves, with an advertising business growing in the 20%s for multiple quarters (far ahead that of Google, its closest competitors in online advertising) and a stock that outperformed both the S&P 500 as well as the Nasdaq 100 in that time frame.

The fact Meta has confirmed they will continue investing in AI via heavy CAPEX allocation is testament to the fact they are walking the talk. I expect Meta to keep delivering value for shareholders going forward, and that’s why I rate it a Buy. Should the company manage not only to keep growing at current rates, but to diversify its business away from Advertising as a result of its AI effort, I would expect the shares to offer outsized returns going forward.

data.title: Dow Jones And Major U.S. Indices Drop From Key Levels - Technical Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Despite a strong open and a decent Jobless Claims release, Equity markets are rejcting their higher levels. The narrative had been confusing since last Friday's Non-Farm payrolls miss:

All indices rallied above their pre-NFP levels in what seemed to be a total discounting of that new information, allowing participants to suppose that the repricing of a September cut (currently 91.5% priced in for 25 bps) would overtake the more pessimistic outlooks for Economy.Was the few days of price action a bull trap? Only the future will tell.

But one sure thing, is that single days of price action are not enough to look at the global picture, a good reason to always take a step back and look at higher timeframes.It is costly to be bearish on Equities which are growing stronger every month. However, with tariffs now in place and data deceleration, it is essential to approach Markets with more caution and treat risk-management measures with importance.

On Monday, we looked at gold and oil performance showing a different story than stocks—commodities are good indicators of actual economic demand and it's good to look at their evolution.

Markets may also look at the continuing claims being at their highest level since November 2021 – It will be essential to see how this story evolves.Let's take a look at charts from the Dow Jones, S&P 500 and Nasdaq to see what technicals are showing.

Dow Jones 8H Chart

The Dow initially bounced higher from oversold NFP levels all the way back to the July range Pivot Zone (44,400 to 44,500).

These mid-range zones tend to be price magnets for retracements, as lots of volumes get trades there. The rebound stopped at the 38.2% Fib of the move down that started just a week ago, with daily highs at 44,519 (CFD, actual index 44,498). The current 8H candle is a strong bearish one, taking the index right around the middle of its longer run downwards channel – Buyers will want to step in around here to avoid sellers keeping their strong hand. Levels to keep in check :

Resistance Levels

  • 44,100 (+/- 3 points) intraday resistance
  • 44,400 to 44,500 Pivot turned Resistance
  • All-time high resistance zone around 45,000

Support Levels

  • Current Pivot 43,500 to 43,750
  • NFP Lows Mini-Support (43,250
  • 43,000 Main Support Zone
  • 42,000 June post-war Support

S&P 500 8H Chart

The S&P got very close to the 6,400 handle with a 6,389 top at the Daily open but is currently down 70 points from there, and was relatively stronger than its big brother, the Dow, as bulls took it to just above the 23.6% of the NFP down-move. RSI Momentum is becoming bearish and sellers pushed the index below its 8H MA 50.

The down session from two days ago stalled at the 6,300 landmark so it will be essential to watch what the index does when it gets there. Any break below will look to test the NFP lows (6,216). Key levels to place on your charts:

Resistance Levels

  • 6,441 ATH on CFD (6,427 on SPX Index)
  • Opening bell highs 6,404 (CFD, 6,389 on the actual index)
  • FOMC Lows resistance Zone 6,350

Support Levels

  • 6,300 Key Support – Current Pivot (+/- 15 points)
  • NFP Lows and lower bound of May Channel – 6,220 to 6,240
  • 6,152 January ATH level

Nasdaq 8H Chart

The Nasdaq saw the biggest reversal, going from up 0.40% on the session to down 0.22% after rejecting the 23,500 zone. The ongoing selling is currently stalling before the 23,215 50-period MA which will require a bullish impulse to avoid, once again, sellers keeping their current strong hand. One major level to look to hold the higher timeframe uptrend is the lower bound of the May lows ascending channel. RSI momentum seems to be turning lower but is still above the neutral line, a development to keep an eye on. Levels to watch:

Resistance levels

  • 23,732 Current all-time highs
  • 23,500 psychological resistance zone
  • Daily highs 23,578 (CFD, actual index 23,560)

Support levels

  • Pivot Zone at 23,150
  • Lower bound of ascending channel 22,915
  • 22,700 Support

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Flowserve: Continuing Alone Without Chart
data.description:
Early in June, I believed that investors in Flowserve (NYSE:FLS) were cautious on the announced merger with Chart Industries (GTLS). Forwarding two months in time, Flowserve´s investors have to focus on the standalone investment case after a competing offering for Chart rightfully resulted in Flowserve calling the merger off, which makes for an interesting reimbursement on its way.

Shares of Flowserve have recovered from the initial negative reaction here, yet amidst uninspiring second quarter results, which are actually indicative of its long-term performance, a fair to low valuation applied to Flowserve does not automatically make me upbeat here.

A Merger Of Equals

In June, both firms announced their intention to merge in what can best be described as a merger of equals. While strategic arguments, which claimed greater diversification and of course synergies, looked good on paper, investors were very cautious. This was evident as shares of both firms fell upon the announcement.

That can be understood as Flowserve, as the surviving entity would see a jump in leverage, while investors in Chart would merge their higher growth prospects into the slower growth prospects of Flowserve. Deal terms called for Flowserve to issue 3.165 shares of its stock for every share outstanding of Chart, which would give investors in Chart a combined 53% equity stake in the business.

By merging Flowserve´s $4.6 billion business, which focuses on pumps, seals, control valves, and Chart's $4.2 billion business, which focuses on heat exchanges, compressors, cryogenic storage, etc., the idea was to create an $8.8 billion diversified industrial energy giant. This business was expected to deliver on $300 million in synergies over time, as Flowserve's margins and growth prospects lagged that of Chart substantially.

Shares of Flowserve fell $3 to $47 per share following the announcement of the deal, as valuations, including the to-be-issued shares, were down about $800 million. Shares of Chart fell some 8% to $148 per share as investors in both firms missed the strategic rationale behind the deal yet failed to have conviction to buy the dip.

Failed Merger, Happy Stock

By the end of July, Flowserve announced that it had terminated the anticipated merger with Chart, this news being communicated alongside the release of the second quarter results.

This followed Chart Industries obtaining an unsolicited acquisition proposal from Baker Hughes (BKR), which was both superior in terms of value and certainty, this being a $210 cash offer. Furthermore, frankly, there seems better overlap between these two firms.

As a result, Flowserve decided not to compete with Baker Hughes, making Flowserve eligible to receive a $266 million termination payment from Chart. With a share count of 131 million shares outstanding, that payment is equal to about $2 per share, yet this is not a free lunch, with the company having incurred expenses as well in relation to the merger, while the business has seen real operational distraction as well.

At the same time, the company reported a near 3% increase in second quarter sales to $1.19 billion, yet organic sales were down a 1% as growth was driven by favorable currency moves, but moreover the impact of acquisitions. However, adjusted margins rose by two points to 14.6% of sales, boosting adjusted earnings by eighteen cents to $0.91 per share.

The gap with GAAP earnings of $0.62 per share is largely driven by costs related to acquisitions, such as amortization and intangible charges, as well as a $0.09 per share charge related to costs incurred with the Chart merger. Somewhat concerning was that bookings only came in at $1.07 billion, and while that ate into the backlog, this backlog of $2.85 billion was still up 6% on the year before.

In terms of the balance sheet, net debt of $856 million was pretty manageable. Second quarter results were somewhat uninspiring from a booking and sales number perspective, although margins were good. This made that the company actually hiked the full-year earnings guidance from $3.10-$3.30 towards $3.25-$3.40 per share. This is even as the higher end of the full-year organic growth guidance was cut by a point to 4%.

Shares have recovered from a deal-induced decline to $47 early in June to $53 at this point in time, driven by a market recovery as well as a hike in the full-year guidance and termination fee receivable. At current levels, multiples look quite fair, with shares trading around 16 times adjusted earnings and the balance sheet being in solid shape.

What Now?

With Flowserve displaying discipline here after a big merger attempt, I like the decisions made by management, as focus on bolt-on dealmaking seems a better idea combined with focus on execution on its own assets. Looking at Flowserve in isolation, we will see a larger competitor if Chart gets sold to Baker Hughes, although that is not really a pure competitor here, so that is not a prime concern.

However, it likely puts pressure on Flowserve to continue to engage at a higher pace in M&A, as Flowserve is simply muddling through here, with a $4.5 billion business in 2024 posting similar sales as it did a decade ago, with investors essentially seeing no growth amidst flattish margins and a flattish share count.

This leaves Flowserve to trade at 16 times adjusted earnings multiples for an earnings yield around 6%, while debt is moderate. This makes it hard to be really upbeat, as the valuations are fair to cheap, but the (past) performance has been uninspiring as well. As a result, I see shares trading in my neutral territory, as I am happy to update the story from here (likely on M&A moves), but the real organic growth pace is what needs addressing here.

data.title: This Market Is Clueless: Why I'm Still All In On Texas Pacific
data.description:
Introduction

There are not many stocks I have mentioned as much as Texas Pacific Land (NYSE:TPL) and LandBridge (LB), which have become my third-largest and largest holdings, respectively.

The reason I'm bringing them up so much isn't to "pump" them, as I doubt I can influence the stock prices of multi-billion dollar companies, anyway, but because they touch on so many things that I'm bullish on.

For example, on July 21, I wrote an article titled "Betting The Ranch: My 2 Largest Investments Just Got Even Better," which discussed a number of secular growth tailwinds, including just how power hungry data centers have become. Between 2020 and 2030, global data center power demand is expected to rise to almost 1,000 TWh, a surge of nearly 5x.

The second tailwind is the one big bottleneck pressuring America's energy industry. That bottleneck is produced water in the Permian Basin. As some of you may know, the Permian Basin is the largest oil basin in the United States.

The basin produces roughly as much oil as all other basins combined.

It's also the only major basin capable of production growth, which explains the widening output gap since the 2020 pandemic, as we can see below.

While the Permian is seeing pressure from falling oil prices and deteriorating Tier 1 reserves, the biggest problem is produced water, which is a byproduct of oil and gas production in the form of toxic water that needs to be dealt with.

In some areas of the Permian (mainly in the Delaware Basin), every barrel of oil comes with up to ten barrels of water. As oil producers are drilling deeper, water is only becoming a bigger problem, leading to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas reporting that the majority of oil companies see long-term constraints from this water problem.

Not to sound dramatic, but given that the Permian was the core of the shale revolution that provided the world with nearly unprecedented oil supply growth between the Great Financial Crisis and the pandemic, Permian water is a global bottleneck problem that needs to be taken care of.

As I showed in that article, drillers are willing to pay a lot of money for water disposal, as prices have risen from roughly $0.15 in 2010 to $1.20 in 2025.

The third tailwind is that I consider energy stocks, in general, to be too cheap and a perfect asset in light of "higher for longer" inflation and interest rate risks. Also, given that money managers are massively underweight in energy, I expect a substantial boost the moment we get some support from cyclical growth.

All of this is bullish for Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge.

Unfortunately, their stock price performances this year haven't reflected any of this good news as their stock prices are 50% (for TPL) and 35% (for LB) below their all-time highs. For TPL, it's the third-worst correction (crash?) of the past 10 years, as the chart below visualizes quite well.

While I have a thick skin, I have to say that it annoys me.

Or, as I wrote in many comments, "I'm 100% annoyed but 0% worried."

However, it makes assessing their quarterly numbers even more important, which is why I decided to fully dedicate this article to Texas Pacific and its just-released earnings.

As we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

I Don't Care What The Market Thinks, This Was An Awesome Quarter

I have to admit that the title above may sound like it was written by someone who is now doing whatever it takes to make his investment look good.

However, the truth is different.

After all, I do not benefit from sugarcoating anything. It would only hurt my own net worth, given the size of my TPL investment and tarnish my credibility, something I have spent more than a decade building.

So, let me explain what I mean by this subheader.

For starters, I believe the single-biggest reason why Texas Pacific is down is the low price of oil. Currently, WTI crude oil is trading for $67 per barrel, a price that's substantially off its lows this year but still a number that causes oil executives in the Lone Star State to play defense.

After all, using Dallas Fed data, we can assume most drillers need prices of $70 to $75 to pull the trigger on higher output. While $67 per barrel means Permian producers can profitably drill new wells, the margin is slim.

Also, oil is down due to OPEC-related uncertainty, poor cyclical growth conditions, tariffs impacting drilling costs and economic growth, and general uncertainty surrounding the energy policy of President Donald Trump.

That's why producers have been dropping rigs.

As we can see below, the total rig is roughly 20% below its 2024 average.

So, needless to say, when bears say this is bad for Texas Pacific, they are not wrong. It's a valid argument and one of the biggest risks for TPL, in general.

However, it's just one part of the story.

For example, the rig count is roughly half of what it was in 2019. Despite that, total oil production has consistently made new highs, supported by the ever-increasing efficiency of American onshore drillers.

All of this is reflected in TPL's quarterly numbers as its total water sales dropped to 482 thousand barrels per day. That's down from 800 thousand barrels per day in 2Q24 and a result of lower drilling activity.

The fewer wells are drilled, the less freshwater is needed.

Also, oil realizations dropped to $63.99 per barrel, down from nearly $81.00 per barrel in the second quarter of 2024. This decline is obvious.

Now comes the good news.

Despite all of this bad news, Texas Pacific Land reported $188 million in revenues, a 9% increase compared to 2Q24, when it generated $172 million.

Wait, what?

Higher revenues are not the result of smart financial engineering but because total oil and gas royalty production rose to 33.2 thousand barrels per day. Bear in mind that this is the share of production it gets from drillers on its land. Texas Pacific does not produce a single drop of oil.

It also saw a new record in produced water royalties to 4,248 thousand barrels per day, a substantial increase from 3,321 thousand barrels in 2Q24.

This allowed the company to maintain an EBITDA margin of 89% and 69% free cash flow margins, making it one of the three most efficient S&P 500 companies.

These numbers underscore the extreme resilience of its "customers" (see above) and the quality of the land it owns. While the number of total rigs has dropped, total permits and drilled but uncompleted wells remain strong.

As we can see above, Permian production has done very well in recent years, despite a lower rig count. Going forward, I expect the rig count to remain stable and most upside to come from production efficiencies and higher water volumes (both fresh water sales and produced water royalties).

During its earnings call, the company addressed the notion that the Permian Basin production is nearing a plateau or decline. Management argued that this perception conflates a temporary slowdown due to low oil prices with geological exhaustion.

The Permian, which spans millions of acres across West Texas and New Mexico, remains the world's largest oil and gas basin, producing 6.5 million barrels of oil per day (about half of U.S. oil production) and 3 million barrels per day of natural gas liquids, accounting for 9% of global liquids supply.

While the growth fears I just discussed are valid, there's no reason to suggest TPL will run into trouble anytime soon. We will likely see decades of elevated production, rising water demand, and new opportunities as prices rise.

Sure, it's not as great as it was in 2013, but I cannot imagine an oil basin I would rather invest in than the mighty Permian, especially because Texas Pacific doesn't carry the capex risks that its customers deal with, as it simply does not produce oil and gas (see below).

Moreover, to support my point, the company cited a report by Inveris that estimates more than 60,000 drilling locations with breakevens below $60 WTI and $3 natural gas, which represent more than 30 billion barrels of oil.

At the 2024 drilling pace of 5,700 wells per year, this would translate to an 11-year inventory for low-breakeven wells, with additional locations becoming economic at higher oil prices.

More Growth Opportunities

One of the things I mentioned a few times in this report is advanced drilling.

This includes horseshoe drilling. This method is called a horseshoe because of the U-shaped wells that allow operators to maximize lateral length within constrained leasehold boundaries. Essentially, it reduces their surface area and capital costs.

There's not a lot of info online, as this is a rather new technology. Currently, Texas Pacific Land has 48 horseshoe wells on its land. Three years ago, that number was zero.

Moreover, the Permian is home to tens of thousands of older wells that were all completed with less efficient technologies. All of these could be re-drilled in the future, especially if higher oil prices make it more economically viable.

So far, so good.

Until this point, I have mainly explained why the Permian Basin remains a great place to be and why Texas Pacific Land was able to defy weakness in 2Q25.

I'm even more upbeat about water.

Currently, the Permian produces more than 23 million barrels of produced water every single day, a number that's expected to substantially increase due to maturing wells and an increasingly unfavorable water-to-oil ratio for drillers.

Texas Pacific has three ways to benefit from this:

  • In-basin disposal capacity: TPL's surface acreage retains millions of barrels per day of disposal capacity, with a conservative approach to signing disposal wells to preserve injection capacity in light of increasing regulatory scrutiny.
  • Out-of-basin disposal: TPL has acquired tens of thousands of acres of out-of-basin pore space. Currently, it's injecting more than 100,000 barrels per day, with plans for further growth this number. Going forward, I expect a lot more out-of-basin disposal, which should benefit pipeline easement income, as midstream companies will have to provide the necessary infrastructure on TPL and third-party land.
  • Desalination and beneficial reuse: As I have written in prior articles, Texas Pacific is working on water cleaning technologies. The desalination facility aims to reduce subsurface injection while producing fresh water for industrial uses like power generation, data center cooling, or hydrogen production. Essentially, this way, the company can benefit from water more than three times as it sells fresh water, cleans produced water, and uses this for value-adding projects. While this is a slow process, I'm very bullish on its long-term benefits.

These benefits are often underestimated, as the company, which has not a penny in gross debt and more than $500 million in cash, has doubled its royalty production and sourced water revenue since the start of 2021. During this period, it has tripled its produced water royalty volumes.

The Selloff and Valuation

Currently, TPL is selling off more than 7% while I'm writing this, pushing its stock price below $900 in a move that I blame on uncertainty regarding Permian drilling activity and a misunderstanding of what TPL is all about.

Even after losing half of its value, TPL still trades north of 40x earnings, a number that may seem abysmal to most. However, it's close to its long-term median as it simply deserves a high multiple.

See, on top of not having any debt, it has sky-high margins, as its capex and opex are minimal. It does not produce oil and gas. All it does is allow others to produce on its land and invest in hardware for water sales, among other minor things.

A company trading at 20x earnings can be much more expensive than TPL's 40x multiple as a much bigger share of these earnings will have to be invested in the business again. For TPL, it's almost all free cash flow.

Moreover, going forward, expectations foresee 30% and 18% EPS growth in 2025 and 2026. While these numbers are subject to change, I believe the company has a fair stock price of at least $1,300, based on its current multiple.

As annoying as this oil environment may be, the company has shown what it is capable of. Just imagine what this could mean in an environment where oil creeps up to $85-$90 again.

The future for TPL is bright.

Takeaway

I'm not sugarcoating it. Watching Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge, which I will cover tomorrow, tumble 50% and 35% from their highs is beyond frustrating.

But here's the thing: I think the market is wrong. It usually is during times of falling oil prices. While some weakness is justified, this crash is not.

TPL just reported a quarter that proves its resilience, with higher revenues despite lower oil prices, record water royalties, and sky-high margins (89% EBITDA, 69% FCF).

Even better, the Permian Basin isn't dying. If anything, it's adapting.

Drillers are squeezing more from fewer rigs, and water demand is exploding, which I expect to continue for many years to come.

While oil at $67 hurts, TPL isn't an oil producer, but a landowner with zero debt and more than $500 million in cash.

At 40x earnings, it may not seem cheap. However, unless you believe oil will never recover, I consider it to be a very cheap stock, which is why I stick to a Strong Buy rating.

Risk to My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is a "lower for longer" oil price environment. It would cause oil companies to become even more defensive and TPL's growth to slow. Regulatory risks are subdued, as most plans already foresee more water shipments to out-of-basin spots.

Financial risks are subdued, as TPL has no debt and no long-term contracts with third parties that could hurt it in any way.

Hence, as I said, I'm 100% annoyed but 0% worried.
